chapter.title: Electromagnetic Induction
chapter.summary:
Chapter 6, Electromagnetic Induction, explores the relationship between magnetism and electricity. It begins by introducing the historical context and defining electromagnetic induction: the generation of electric current by changing magnetic fields. The chapter then delves into Faraday and Henry's experiments, which form the basis for understanding this phenomenon.

The concept of magnetic flux, a measure of the magnetic field passing through a surface, is introduced and defined. Faraday's Law is presented, stating that the induced electromotive force (EMF) is proportional to the rate of change of magnetic flux. The mathematical formulation, ε = -N(dΦB/dt), and its implications are discussed, including motional EMF and induced electric fields.

The chapter transitions to inductance, covering mutual and self-inductance. Mutual inductance explains how a changing current in one coil induces an EMF in a neighboring coil, while self-inductance describes the EMF induced in a single coil due to its own changing current. The chapter concludes with the AC generator, a practical application of electromagnetic induction. It explains how rotating a coil in a magnetic field produces alternating current. The chapter ends by summarizing key laws and definitions and providing conceptual insights. The exercises then task the student with applying these laws to different problems.

subsections.title: Historical Context of Electricity and Magnetism
subsections.content_text: For a long time, electricity and magnetism were considered separate and unrelated phenomena. In the early decades of the nineteenth century, experiments by Oersted, Ampere, and others established that they are inter-related, showing that moving electric charges produce magnetic fields. For example, an electric current deflects a magnetic compass needle placed in its vicinity. This discovery naturally raised the question of whether the converse is possible: can moving magnets produce electric currents? In 1830, Michael Faraday in England and Joseph Henry in the USA independently demonstrated that electric currents are induced in closed coils when subjected to changing magnetic fields. This discovery is of immense practical utility; without it, we would lack electric lights, trains, telephones, and personal computers. The pioneering work of Faraday and Henry led directly to the development of modern generators and transformers, which are the backbone of modern civilization. When Faraday first made public his discovery that relative motion between a bar magnet and a wire loop produced a small current, he was asked, "What is the use of it?" His reply was: "What is the use of a new born baby?"

subsections.title: Definition of Electromagnetic Induction
subsections.content_text: The phenomenon in which electric current is generated by varying magnetic fields is appropriately called electromagnetic induction. In this chapter, we will study the phenomena associated with changing magnetic fields and understand the underlying principles.

sections.title: The Experiments of Faraday and Henry

subsections.title: Experimental Foundation and Key Figures
subsections.content_text: The discovery and understanding of electromagnetic induction are based on a long series of experiments carried out by Faraday and Henry. Note that wherever the term 'coil' or 'loop' is used, it is assumed they are made of conducting material and prepared using wires coated with insulating material. Joseph Henry (1797-1878), an American experimental physicist and first director of the Smithsonian Institution, made important improvements in electromagnets by winding coils of insulated wire around iron pole pieces and invented an electromagnetic motor and a new, efficient telegraph. He discovered self-induction and investigated how currents in one circuit induce currents in another.

subsections.title: Experiment 6.1: Induction by a Moving Magnet
subsections.content_text: In this experiment, a coil $C_1$ is connected to a galvanometer $G$. When the North-pole of a bar magnet is pushed towards the coil, the galvanometer pointer deflects, indicating the presence of electric current in the coil. This deflection lasts only as long as the bar magnet is in motion. When the magnet is held stationary, the galvanometer does not show any deflection. If the magnet is pulled away from the coil, the galvanometer shows deflection in the opposite direction, indicating reversal of the current's direction. Using the South-pole of the bar magnet results in deflections opposite to those observed with the North-pole for similar movements. The deflection (and hence current) is larger when the magnet is moved faster. If the bar magnet is held fixed and the coil $C_1$ is moved towards or away from the magnet, the same effects are observed. This shows that it is the relative motion between the magnet and the coil that is responsible for the generation (induction) of electric current in the coil.

diagrams.label: Figure 6.1
diagrams.description: A bar magnet with North (N) and South (S) poles is being pushed towards a coil $C_1$. The coil is connected to a galvanometer $G$. Magnetic field lines are shown emanating from the magnet and passing through the coil. The galvanometer shows a deflection $I$.

subsections.title: Experiment 6.2: Induction by a Moving Current-Carrying Coil
subsections.content_text: In this experiment, the bar magnet is replaced by a second coil $C_2$ connected to a battery. The steady current in coil $C_2$ produces a steady magnetic field. As coil $C_2$ is moved towards coil $C_1$, the galvanometer shows a deflection, indicating that electric current is induced in coil $C_1$. When $C_2$ is moved away, the galvanometer shows a deflection again, but in the opposite direction. The deflection lasts as long as coil $C_2$ is in motion. If coil $C_2$ is held fixed and $C_1$ is moved, the same effects are observed. This confirms that it is the relative motion between the coils that induces the electric current.

diagrams.label: Figure 6.2
diagrams.description: Two coils $C_1$ and $C_2$ are shown. Coil $C_1$ is connected to a galvanometer $G$. Coil $C_2$ is connected to a battery and is being moved towards $C_1$. Magnetic field lines from $C_2$ pass through $C_1$, and the galvanometer shows a deflection $I$.

subsections.title: Experiment 6.3: Induction by Changing Current in Stationary Coils
subsections.content_text: Faraday showed that relative motion is not an absolute requirement for induction. In this experiment, two coils $C_1$ and $C_2$ are held stationary. Coil $C_1$ is connected to a galvanometer $G$, while coil $C_2$ is connected to a battery through a tapping key $K$. It is observed that the galvanometer shows a momentary deflection when the tapping key $K$ is pressed. The pointer returns to zero immediately even if the key is held pressed continuously. When the key is released, a momentary deflection is observed again, but in the opposite direction. It is also observed that the deflection increases dramatically when an iron rod is inserted into the coils along their axis, which enhances the magnetic coupling between them.

diagrams.label: Figure 6.3
diagrams.description: Two stationary coils $C_1$ and $C_2$. Coil $C_1$ is connected to a galvanometer $G$. Coil $C_2$ is connected to a battery through a tapping key $K$. The diagram shows the setup for observing induction without relative motion by opening and closing the circuit.

subsections.title: Introduction to Magnetic Flux
subsections.content_text: Faraday's great insight lay in discovering a simple mathematical relation to explain the series of experiments he carried out on electromagnetic induction. Before stating and appreciating his laws, we must get familiar with the notion of magnetic flux, $\Phi_B$. Magnetic flux is defined in the same way as electric flux is defined in Chapter 1. Magnetic flux through a plane of area $A$ placed in a uniform magnetic field $\mathbf{B}$ (Fig. 6.4) can be written as $\Phi_B = \mathbf{B} \cdot \mathbf{A} = BA \cos \theta$, where $\theta$ is the angle between the magnetic field $\mathbf{B}$ and the area vector $\mathbf{A}$. The concept of area as a vector, discussed in earlier chapters, allows this definition to be extended to curved surfaces and non-uniform fields.

diagrams.label: FIGURE 6.4
diagrams.description: A diagram showing a flat rectangular surface of area vector A oriented at an angle theta to uniform magnetic field lines B.

subsections.title: Magnetic Flux in Non-uniform Fields
subsections.content_text: For surfaces where the magnetic field varies in magnitude or direction, the total magnetic flux is the sum of the flux through all infinitesimal area elements $d\mathbf{A}_i$ that make up the surface. This is represented as $\Phi_B = \mathbf{B}_1 \cdot d\mathbf{A}_1 + \mathbf{B}_2 \cdot d\mathbf{A}_2 + \dots = \sum_{all} \mathbf{B}_i \cdot d\mathbf{A}_i$, where $\mathbf{B}_i$ is the specific magnetic field at each element.

diagrams.label: FIGURE 6.5
diagrams.description: A diagram of an irregular curved surface divided into small area elements, each with its own area vector dA_i and local magnetic field vector B_i.

subsections.title: Units and Properties of Magnetic Flux
subsections.content_text: Magnetic flux is a scalar quantity. Its SI unit is the weber (Wb), which is equivalent to a tesla meter squared ($T m^2$).

subsections.title: Faraday's Law of Induction: Introduction
subsections.content_text: Based on experimental results, Faraday determined that an electromotive force (emf) is induced in a coil whenever the magnetic flux passing through it changes over time. This principle provides a theoretical framework for the experimental results observed in Faraday and Henry's work involving moving magnets and varying currents.

subsections.title: Mechanism of Flux Change in Experiments
subsections.content_text: In experiments where a magnet or a current-carrying coil is moved relative to another coil, the magnetic flux associated with the stationary coil changes, inducing an emf. Similarly, in a setup with a tapping key, the rapid rise or fall of current upon pressing or releasing the key changes the magnetic field and flux in the neighboring coil, inducing a momentary current. It is important to note that rapid changes in flux can generate high emfs that might damage sensitive electrical equipment.

subsections.title: Statement of Faraday's Law
subsections.content_text: Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction states that the magnitude of the induced emf in a circuit is equal to the time rate of change of magnetic flux through that circuit.

subsections.title: Mathematical Formulation of Faraday's Law
subsections.content_text: The induced emf $\varepsilon$ is mathematically expressed as $\varepsilon = -\frac{d\Phi_B}{dt}$. The negative sign in the equation indicates the direction of the induced emf and the resulting current, which determines the polarity of the induced voltage in a closed loop.

subsections.title: EMF in Multi-turn Coils
subsections.content_text: In a coil with $N$ turns that are wound closely together, the change in magnetic flux is the same for every turn. The total induced emf is therefore the sum of the emfs in each individual turn: $\varepsilon = -N\frac{d\Phi_B}{dt}$. This relationship shows that increasing the number of turns is an effective method for boosting the induced emf.

subsections.title: Variables Influencing Induced EMF
subsections.content_text: Magnetic flux can be altered by changing the magnetic field $\mathbf{B}$, the area $\mathbf{A}$ of the coil (by deformation such as stretching or shrinking), or the orientation $\theta$ between them (by rotation). Any change in these parameters results in an induced emf in the circuit, as seen in experiments where coils are moved or rotated in magnetic fields.

subsections.title: Example 6.1: Practical Methods for Induction
subsections.content_text: This example explores practical ways to enhance induced emf and methods to detect it without standard laboratory equipment like a galvanometer.

worked_examples.label: Example 6.1
worked_examples.problem: Consider Experiment 6.2. (a) What would you do to obtain a large deflection of the galvanometer? (b) How would you demonstrate the presence of an induced current in the absence of a galvanometer?
worked_examples.solution: (a) To obtain a large deflection, one or more of the following steps can be taken: (i) Use a rod made of soft iron inside the coil $C_2$, (ii) Connect the coil to a powerful battery, and (iii) Move the arrangement rapidly towards the test coil $C_1$. (b) Replace the galvanometer by a small bulb, the kind one finds in a small torch light. The relative motion between the two coils will cause the bulb to glow and thus demonstrate the presence of an induced current.

subsections.title: Example 6.2: Calculating EMF in a Square Loop
subsections.content_text: This example demonstrates the calculation of induced emf and current in a square loop when the magnetic field is reduced to zero over a specific time interval.

worked_examples.label: Example 6.2
worked_examples.problem: A square loop of side $10$ cm and resistance $0.5 \Omega$ is placed vertically in the east-west plane. A uniform magnetic field of $0.10$ T is set up across the plane in the north-east direction. The magnetic field is decreased to zero in $0.70$ s at a steady rate. Determine the magnitudes of induced emf and current during this time-interval.
worked_examples.solution: The angle $\theta$ made by the area vector of the coil with the magnetic field is $45^\circ$. From Eq. (6.1), the initial magnetic flux is $\Phi = BA \cos \theta = \frac{0.1 \times 10^{-2}}{\sqrt{2}}$ Wb. Final flux, $\Phi_{min} = 0$. The change in flux is brought about in $0.70$ s. From Eq. (6.3), the magnitude of the induced emf is given by $\varepsilon = \frac{|\Delta \Phi_B|}{\Delta t} = \frac{|(\Phi - 0)|}{\Delta t} = \frac{10^{-3}}{\sqrt{2} \times 0.7} = 1.0$ mV. And the magnitude of the current is $I = \frac{\varepsilon}{R} = \frac{10^{-3} V}{0.5 \Omega} = 2$ mA. Note that the earth's magnetic field also produces a flux through the loop. But it is a steady field (which does not change within the time span of the experiment) and hence does not induce any emf.

subsections.title: Example 6.3: EMF from a Rotating Coil
subsections.content_text: This example estimates the average induced emf and current in a multi-turn circular coil rotated within the Earth's magnetic field.

worked_examples.label: Example 6.3
worked_examples.problem: A circular coil of radius $10$ cm, $500$ turns and resistance $2 \Omega$ is placed with its plane perpendicular to the horizontal component of the earth's magnetic field. It is rotated about its vertical diameter through $180^\circ$ in $0.25$ s. Estimate the magnitudes of the emf and current induced in the coil. Horizontal component of the earth's magnetic field at the place is $3.0 \times 10^{-5}$ T.
worked_examples.solution: Initial flux through the coil, $\Phi_{B(initial)} = BA \cos \theta = 3.0 \times 10^{-5} \times (\pi \times 10^{-2}) \times \cos 0^\circ = 3\pi \times 10^{-7}$ Wb. Final flux after the rotation, $\Phi_{B(final)} = 3.0 \times 10^{-5} \times (\pi \times 10^{-2}) \times \cos 180^\circ = -3\pi \times 10^{-7}$ Wb. Therefore, estimated value of the induced emf is, $\varepsilon = N \frac{\Delta \Phi}{\Delta t} = 500 \times (6\pi \times 10^{-7})/0.25 = 3.8 \times 10^{-3}$ V. $I = \varepsilon/R = 1.9 \times 10^{-3}$ A. Note that the magnitudes of $\varepsilon$ and $I$ are the estimated values. Their instantaneous values are different and depend upon the speed of rotation at the particular instant.

sections.title: Lenz's Law and Conservation of Energy

subsections.title: Statement of Lenz's Law
subsections.content_text:
In 1834, German physicist Heinrich Friedrich Lenz (1804-1865) deduced a rule, known as Lenz's law, which gives the polarity of the induced emf in a clear and concise fashion. The statement of the law is:

**The polarity of induced emf is such that it tends to produce a current which opposes the change in magnetic flux that produced it.**

The negative sign in Faraday's law $\varepsilon = -\frac{d\Phi_B}{dt}$ represents this effect.

subsections.title: Understanding Lenz's Law through Experiments
subsections.content_text:
We can understand Lenz's law by examining Experiment 6.1 in Section 6.2.1. When the North-pole of a bar magnet is pushed towards a closed coil, the magnetic flux through the coil increases. Hence current is induced in the coil in such a direction that it opposes the increase in flux. This is possible only if the current in the coil is in a counter-clockwise direction with respect to an observer situated on the side of the magnet. The magnetic moment associated with this current has North polarity towards the North-pole of the approaching magnet.

Similarly, if the North-pole of the magnet is being withdrawn from the coil, the magnetic flux through the coil will decrease. To counter this decrease in magnetic flux, the induced current in the coil flows in clockwise direction and its South-pole faces the receding North-pole of the bar magnet. This would result in an attractive force which opposes the motion of the magnet and the corresponding decrease in flux.

If an open circuit is used instead of a closed loop, an emf is still induced across the open ends. The direction of the induced emf can be found using Lenz's law.

diagrams.label: Fig 6.6
diagrams.description: Diagrams showing the direction of induced currents when a bar magnet is pushed towards and pulled away from a coil, illustrating Lenz's law.

subsections.title: Lenz's Law and Conservation of Energy
subsections.content_text:
Suppose that the induced current was in the direction opposite to the one depicted in Fig. 6.6(a). In that case, the South-pole due to the induced current will face the approaching North-pole of the magnet. The bar magnet will then be attracted towards the coil at an ever increasing acceleration. A gentle push on the magnet would initiate the process and its velocity and kinetic energy would continuously increase without expending any energy. If this were possible, one could construct a perpetual-motion machine by a suitable arrangement. This violates the law of conservation of energy and hence cannot happen.

In the correct case, the bar magnet experiences a repulsive force due to the induced current. Therefore, a person has to do work in moving the magnet. This energy is dissipated by Joule heating produced by the induced current. Lenz's law is thus consistent with the principle of conservation of energy.

worked_examples.label: Example 6.4
worked_examples.problem: Figure 6.7 shows planar loops of different shapes moving out of or into a region of a magnetic field which is directed normal to the plane of the loop away from the reader. Determine the direction of induced current in each loop using Lenz's law.
worked_examples.solution:
(i) The magnetic flux through the rectangular loop $abcd$ increases, due to the motion of the loop into the region of magnetic field. The induced current must flow along the path $bcdab$ so that it opposes the increasing flux.
(ii) Due to the outward motion, magnetic flux through the triangular loop $abc$ decreases due to which the induced current flows along $bacb$, so as to oppose the change in flux.
(iii) As the magnetic flux decreases due to motion of the irregular shaped loop $abcd$ out of the region of magnetic field, the induced current flows along $cdabc$, so as to oppose change in flux.
Note that there are no induced currents as long as the loops are completely inside or outside the region of the magnetic field.

worked_examples.label: Example 6.5
worked_examples.problem:
(a) A closed loop is held stationary in the magnetic field between the north and south poles of two permanent magnets held fixed. Can we hope to generate current in the loop by using very strong magnets?
(b) A closed loop moves normal to the constant electric field between the plates of a large capacitor. Is a current induced in the loop (i) when it is wholly inside the region between the capacitor plates (ii) when it is partially outside the plates of the capacitor? The electric field is normal to the plane of the loop.
(c) A rectangular loop and a circular loop are moving out of a uniform magnetic field region to a field-free region with a constant velocity $\mathbf{v}$. In which loop do you expect the induced emf to be constant during the passage out of the field region? The field is normal to the loops.
worked_examples.solution:
(a) No. However strong the magnet may be, current can be induced only by changing the magnetic flux through the loop. Since the magnetic field is constant (both in magnitude and direction) and the loop is stationary, the flux through the loop remains constant, and no current is induced.
(b) No current is induced in either case. A constant electric field does not produce a changing magnetic flux. The electric flux through the loop changes but there is no relation analogous to Faraday's law for electric flux.
(c) The induced emf is constant for the rectangular loop because a constant length of the conductor is always in the field region as it exits. For the circular loop, the length of the conductor within the field region varies as it moves out, leading to a non-constant emf.

diagrams.label: Fig 6.7
diagrams.description: Planar loops of various shapes (rectangular, triangular, irregular) moving in and out of a region of uniform magnetic field directed into the page.

diagrams.label: Fig 6.8
diagrams.description: A rectangular loop and a circular loop moving out of a uniform magnetic field region with constant velocity.

subsections.title: Motional Electromotive Force
subsections.content_text:
Consider a straight conductor moving in a uniform and time-independent magnetic field. Figure 6.5 shows a rectangular conductor $PQRS$ in which conductor $PQ$ is free to move. The rod $PQ$ is moved towards the left with a constant velocity $v$. The magnetic field $\mathbf{B}$ points into the page. As the rod moves, the free charges in the rod also move with speed $v$ in the magnetic field $\mathbf{B}$.

Let the rod $PQ$ be moved to the left, decreasing the area of the loop. If the length of $PQ$ is $l$ and it is moved through a small distance $dx$ in time $dt$, the change in area is $dA = l\, dx$. The change in magnetic flux is $d\Phi_B = B\, l\, dx$. The induced emf is:

$$\varepsilon = -\frac{d\Phi_B}{dt} = Blv$$

where we have used $dx/dt = -v$ which is the speed of the conductor $PQ$. The induced emf $Blv$ is called motional emf. Thus, we are able to produce induced emf by moving a conductor instead of varying the magnetic field, that is, by changing the magnetic flux enclosed by the circuit.

subsections.title: Lorentz Force Explanation of Motional EMF
subsections.content_text: It is also possible to explain the motional emf expression by invoking the Lorentz force acting on the free charge carriers of conductor $PQ$. Consider any arbitrary charge $q$ in the conductor $PQ$. When the rod moves with speed $v$, the charge will also be moving with speed $v$ in the magnetic field $\mathbf{B}$. The Lorentz force on this charge is $qvB$ in magnitude, and its direction is towards $Q$. All charges experience the same force, in magnitude and direction, irrespective of their position in the rod $PQ$. The work done in moving the charge from $P$ to $Q$ is $W = qvBl$. Since emf is the work done per unit charge, $\epsilon = W/q = Blv$. This equation gives emf induced across the rod $PQ$ and is identical to the expression derived from Faraday's law. While this presentation is not wholly rigorous, it helps understand the basis of Faraday’s law when the conductor is moving in a uniform and time-independent magnetic field.

subsections.title: Induced Electric Fields from Time-Varying Magnetic Fields
subsections.content_text: On the other hand, it is not obvious how an emf is induced when a conductor is stationary and the magnetic field is changing—a fact which Faraday verified by numerous experiments. In the case of a stationary conductor, the force on its charges is given by $\mathbf{F} = q(\mathbf{E} + \mathbf{v} \times \mathbf{B}) = q\mathbf{E}$ since $\mathbf{v} = 0$. Thus, any force on the charge must arise from the electric field term $\mathbf{E}$ alone. Therefore, to explain the existence of induced emf or induced current, we must assume that a time-varying magnetic field generates an electric field. However, electric fields produced by time-varying magnetic fields have properties different from those produced by static electric charges. This highlights the fundamental significance of Faraday’s discovery: electricity and magnetism are related. Just as charges in motion (current) can exert force on a magnet, a changing magnetic field can exert a force on a stationary charge.

subsections.title: Example 6.6: Rotating Rod EMF
subsections.content_text: This subsection covers the calculation of emf induced in a metallic rod rotating in a uniform magnetic field. The problem explores two methods: one based on the Lorentz force on individual segments of the rod and another based on the rate of change of area swept by the rod.

worked_examples.label: Example 6.6
worked_examples.problem: A metallic rod of 1 m length is rotated with a frequency of 50 rev/s, with one end hinged at the centre and the other end at the circumference of a circular metallic ring of radius 1 m, about an axis passing through the centre and perpendicular to the plane of the ring (Fig. 6.11). A constant and uniform magnetic field of 1 T parallel to the axis is present everywhere. What is the emf between the centre and the metallic ring?
worked_examples.solution: Method I: As the rod is rotated, free electrons in the rod move towards the outer end due to Lorentz force and get distributed over the ring. Thus, the resulting separation of charges produces an emf across the ends of the rod. At a certain value of emf, there is no more flow of electrons and a steady state is reached. The magnitude of the emf generated across a length $dr$ of the rod as it moves at right angles to the magnetic field is given by $d\epsilon = Bv dr$. Hence, $\epsilon = \int d\epsilon = \int_0^R Bv dr = \int_0^R B \omega r dr = \frac{B \omega R^2}{2}$. Note that we have used $v = \omega r$. This gives $\epsilon = \frac{1}{2} \times 1.0 \times 2\pi \times 50 \times (1^2) = 157$ V. Method II: To calculate the emf, we can imagine a closed loop $OPQ$ in which point $O$ and $P$ are connected with a resistor $R$ and $OQ$ is the rotating rod. The potential difference across the resistor is then equal to the induced emf and equals $B \times$ (rate of change of area of loop). If $\theta$ is the angle between the rod and the radius of the circle at $P$ at time $t$, the area of the sector $OPQ$ is given by $\pi R^2 \times \frac{\theta}{2\pi} = \frac{1}{2} R^2 \theta$ where $R$ is the radius of the circle. Hence, the induced emf is $\epsilon = B \times \frac{d}{dt} [\frac{1}{2} R^2 \theta] = \frac{1}{2} B R^2 \frac{d\theta}{dt} = \frac{B \omega R^2}{2}$ [Note: $\frac{d\theta}{dt} = \omega = 2\pi \nu$]. This expression is identical to the expression obtained by Method I and we get the same value of $\epsilon$.

diagrams.label: Figure 6.11
diagrams.description: A diagram showing a metallic rod OP of length R rotating in a uniform magnetic field (represented by crosses). The rod is hinged at O and its other end P touches a circular metallic ring. The angle theta = omega*t is shown between the rod and a reference radius OQ.

subsections.title: Example 6.7: Rotating Wheel EMF
subsections.content_text: This subsection applies the formula for induced emf in a rotating conductor to a wheel with multiple spokes rotating in the Earth's magnetic field. It emphasizes that the number of spokes does not affect the total emf because they are connected in parallel.

worked_examples.label: Example 6.7
worked_examples.problem: A wheel with 10 metallic spokes each 0.5 m long is rotated with a speed of 120 rev/min in a plane normal to the horizontal component of earth’s magnetic field $H_E$ at a place. If $H_E = 0.4$ G at the place, what is the induced emf between the axle and the rim of the wheel? Note that 1 G = $10^{-4}$ T.
worked_examples.solution: Induced emf $= (1/2) \omega B R^2 = (1/2) \times 4\pi \times 0.4 \times 10^{-4} \times (0.5)^2 = 6.28 \times 10^{-5}$ V. The number of spokes is immaterial because the emf’s across the spokes are in parallel.

subsections.title: Concept of Inductance and Flux Linkage
subsections.content_text: An electric current can be induced in a coil by flux change produced by another coil in its vicinity or flux change produced by the same coil. In both cases, the flux through a coil is proportional to the current, expressed as $\Phi_B \propto I$. If the geometry of the coil does not vary with time, then $d\Phi_B/dt \propto dI/dt$. For a closely wound coil of $N$ turns, the same magnetic flux is linked with all the turns. When the flux $\Phi_B$ through the coil changes, each turn contributes to the induced emf. Therefore, a term called flux linkage is used which is equal to $N\Phi_B$ for a closely wound coil, and in such a case $N\Phi_B \propto I$. The constant of proportionality in this relation is called inductance.

subsections.title: Physical Properties and Units of Inductance
subsections.content_text: Inductance depends only on the geometry of the coil and intrinsic material properties. This aspect is akin to capacitance which for a parallel plate capacitor depends on the plate area and plate separation (geometry) and the dielectric constant $K$ of the intervening medium (intrinsic material property). Inductance is a scalar quantity. It has the dimensions of $[ML^2T^{-2}A^{-2}]$ given by the dimensions of flux divided by the dimensions of current. The SI unit of inductance is henry and is denoted by H. It is named in honour of Joseph Henry who discovered electromagnetic induction in USA, independently of Faraday in England.

subsections.title: 6.7.1 Mutual Inductance
subsections.content_text: Consider Fig. 6.12 which shows two long co-axial solenoids each of length $l$. We denote the radius of the inner solenoid $S_1$ by $r_1$ and the number of turns per unit length by $n_1$. The corresponding quantities for the outer solenoid $S_2$ are $r_2$ and $n_2$, respectively. Let $N_1$ and $N_2$ be the total number of turns of coils $S_1$ and $S_2$, respectively. When a current $I_2$ is set up through the outer solenoid $S_2$, it in turn sets up a magnetic flux through the inner solenoid $S_1$. Let us denote it by $\Phi_1$. The corresponding flux linkage with solenoid $S_1$ is $N_1 \Phi_1 = M_{12} I_2$ (Eq. 6.7). $M_{12}$ is called the mutual inductance of solenoid $S_1$ with respect to solenoid $S_2$, also referred to as the coefficient of mutual induction. For these simple co-axial solenoids, the magnetic field due to the current $I_2$ in $S_2$ is $\mu_0 n_2 I_2$. The resulting flux linkage with coil $S_1$ is $N_1 \Phi_1 = (n_1 l) (\pi r_1^2) (\mu_0 n_2 I_2) = \mu_0 n_1 n_2 \pi r_1^2 l I_2$ (Eq. 6.8), where $n_1 l$ is the total number of turns in solenoid $S_1$. Thus, $M_{12} = \mu_0 n_1 n_2 \pi r_1^2 l$ (Eq. 6.9). This calculation neglects edge effects and assumes the magnetic field is uniform throughout the length and width of $S_2$, which is a good approximation for long solenoids ($l \gg r_2$). In the reverse case, where a current $I_1$ is passed through solenoid $S_1$, the flux linkage with coil $S_2$ is $N_2 \Phi_2 = M_{21} I_1$ (Eq. 6.10). The flux due to $I_1$ in $S_1$ is confined solely inside $S_1$ as the solenoids are very long. Thus, the flux linkage with solenoid $S_2$ is $N_2 \Phi_2 = (n_2 l) (\pi r_1^2) (\mu_0 n_1 I_1)$, leading to $M_{21} = \mu_0 n_1 n_2 \pi r_1^2 l$ (Eq. 6.11).

diagrams.label: Fig 6.12
diagrams.description: A diagram showing two long co-axial solenoids S1 and S2 of the same length l. S1 is the inner solenoid with radius r1 and N1 turns, while S2 is the outer solenoid with radius r2 and N2 turns. The diagram illustrates the radii and the common length l.

subsections.title: Reciprocity and Factors Affecting Mutual Inductance
subsections.content_text: From the derivations for co-axial solenoids, we find $M_{12} = M_{21} = M$ (Eq. 6.12). This equality is a general property known as the reciprocity theorem. It is particularly useful in situations where calculating one mutual inductance is easier than the other, such as when an inner solenoid is much shorter than the outer one. Mutual inductance depends on the geometry of the coils, their separation, and their relative orientation. If the medium within the solenoids has a relative permeability $\mu_r$ instead of air, the mutual inductance becomes $M = \mu_r \mu_0 n_1 n_2 \pi r_1^2 l$.

subsections.title: Example 6.8: Mutual Inductance of Concentric Coils
subsections.content_text: This example calculates the mutual inductance for two concentric circular coils of radii $r_1$ and $r_2$, where $r_1 \ll r_2$. By assuming a current in the larger coil and calculating the flux through the smaller coil, the mutual inductance is determined using the reciprocity relation.

worked_examples.label: Example 6.8
worked_examples.problem: Two concentric circular coils, one of small radius $r_1$ and the other of large radius $r_2$, such that $r_1 \ll r_2$, are placed co-axially with centres coinciding. Obtain the mutual inductance of the arrangement.
worked_examples.solution: Let a current $I_2$ flow through the outer circular coil. The field at the centre of the coil is $B_2 = \mu_0 I_2 / 2r_2$. Since the other co-axially placed coil has a very small radius, $B_2$ may be considered constant over its cross-sectional area. Hence, $\Phi_1 = \pi r_1^2 B_2 = \frac{\mu_0 \pi r_1^2}{2r_2} I_2 = M_{12} I_2$. Thus, $M_{12} = \frac{\mu_0 \pi r_1^2}{2r_2}$. From Eq. (6.12), $M_{12} = M_{21} = \frac{\mu_0 \pi r_1^2}{2r_2}$. Note that we calculated $M_{12}$ from an approximate value of $\Phi_1$, assuming the magnetic field $B_2$ to be uniform over the area $\pi r_1^2$. However, we can accept this value because $r_1 \ll r_2$.

subsections.title: Induced EMF and Mutual Inductance
subsections.content_text: A varying current in one coil induces an emf in a neighbouring coil. If $\Phi_1$ is the flux through coil $C_1$ due to current $I_2$ in coil $C_2$, then $N_1 \Phi_1 = M I_2$. For currents varying with time, $\frac{d(N_1 \Phi_1)}{dt} = \frac{d(M I_2)}{dt}$. Since the induced emf in coil $C_1$ is $\varepsilon_1 = -\frac{d(N_1 \Phi_1)}{dt}$, we obtain $\varepsilon_1 = -M \frac{dI_2}{dt}$. This shows that the magnitude of the induced emf depends upon the rate of change of current and the mutual inductance of the two coils.

subsections.title: 6.7.2 Self-inductance: Concept and Definition
subsections.content_text: Self-induction is the phenomenon where an emf is induced in a single isolated coil due to a change of flux through the coil caused by varying the current through the same coil. In this case, the flux linkage $N \Phi_B$ through a coil of $N$ turns is proportional to the current $I$ through the coil: $N \Phi_B \propto I$, or $N \Phi_B = L I$ (Eq. 6.13). The constant of proportionality $L$ is called the self-inductance of the coil, also referred to as the coefficient of self-induction. When the current is varied, the induced emf is given by $\varepsilon = -\frac{d(N \Phi_B)}{dt} = -L \frac{dI}{dt}$ (Eq. 6.14). The self-induced emf always opposes any change (increase or decrease) of current in the coil and is therefore called the back emf.

subsections.title: Self-inductance of a Long Solenoid
subsections.content_text: To calculate the self-inductance of a long solenoid of cross-sectional area $A$, length $l$, and $n$ turns per unit length, we use the magnetic field $B = \mu_0 n I$. The total flux linked with the solenoid is $N \Phi_B = (nl) (\mu_0 n I) (A) = \mu_0 n^2 A l I$. Thus, the self-inductance is $L = \frac{N \Phi_B}{I} = \mu_0 n^2 A l$ (Eq. 6.15). If the solenoid is filled with a material of relative permeability $\mu_r$, the self-inductance becomes $L = \mu_r \mu_0 n^2 A l$ (Eq. 6.16). The self-inductance depends on the geometry of the coil and the permeability of the medium. Physically, the self-inductance plays Self-inductance acts as the electromagnetic analogue of mass in mechanics, representing the property of inertia. To establish a current in a circuit, work must be performed against the back electromotive force (emf). This work is stored within the inductor as magnetic potential energy. For a circuit with current $I$ at a specific instant, the rate of work done is given by $\frac{dW}{dt} = |\epsilon| I$. By substituting the expression for inductive emf, $\epsilon = L \frac{dI}{dt}$, the rate of work becomes $\frac{dW}{dt} = L I \frac{dI}{dt}$. The total energy $W$ required to build the current from zero to $I$ is the integral of this power over time: $W = \int_0^I L I dI = \frac{1}{2} L I^2$. This expression is mathematically similar to the kinetic energy of a particle, $\frac{1}{2} m v^2$, highlighting that $L$ opposes changes in current just as mass opposes changes in velocity.

subsections.title: Flux Linkage in Multiple Coils
subsections.content_text: In scenarios where multiple coils are positioned near each other and carry current simultaneously, the total magnetic flux linked with a single coil is the sum of the individual fluxes produced by each coil. For two coils, the flux linkage in the first coil is expressed as $N_1 \Phi_1 = M_{11} I_1 + M_{12} I_2$, where $M_{11}$ represents the self-inductance (often denoted as $L_1$) and $M_{12}$ is the mutual inductance. Applying Faraday's law, the induced emf in the first coil is $\epsilon_1 = -L_1 \frac{dI_1}{dt} - M_{12} \frac{dI_2}{dt}$.

subsections.title: Magnetic Energy and Energy Density
subsections.content_text: The energy stored in a magnetic field can be expressed in terms of the field strength $B$. For a solenoid, the magnetic energy is $U_B = \frac{B^2}{2\mu_0} Al$. When considering the energy per unit volume, known as magnetic energy density $u_B$, the expression becomes $u_B = \frac{B^2}{2\mu_0}$. This is analogous to the electrostatic energy density in a capacitor, $u_E = \frac{1}{2} \epsilon_0 E^2$. In both cases, the energy density is proportional to the square of the field strength. These relations are general and apply to any region of space containing magnetic or electric fields.

worked_examples.label: Example 6.9
worked_examples.problem: (a) Obtain the expression for the magnetic energy stored in a solenoid in terms of magnetic field $B$, area $A$ and length $l$ of the solenoid. (b) How does this magnetic energy compare with the electrostatic energy stored in a capacitor?
worked_examples.solution: (a) From the energy formula $U_B = \frac{1}{2} L I^2$ and the solenoid field $B = \mu_0 n I$, we find $I = \frac{B}{\mu_0 n}$. Substituting $L = \mu_0 n^2 A l$, we get $U_B = \frac{1}{2} (\mu_0 n^2 A l) (\frac{B}{\mu_0 n})^2 = \frac{B^2}{2\mu_0} A l$. (b) The magnetic energy per unit volume is $u_B = \frac{U_B}{V} = \frac{B^2}{2\mu_0}$. Comparing this to the electrostatic energy density $u_E = \frac{1}{2} \epsilon_0 E^2$, we see both are proportional to the square of their respective field strengths.

subsections.title: Principles of Alternating Current Generation
subsections.content_text: The AC generator is a primary technological application of electromagnetic induction, used to generate alternating current (ac). Nicola Tesla is credited with the development of this machine. The basic principle involves rotating a coil within a uniform magnetic field. As the coil rotates, its orientation relative to the field changes, which modifies the effective area $A \cos \theta$ (where $\theta$ is the angle between the magnetic field $\vec{B}$ and the area vector $\vec{A}$). This continuous change in flux induces an electromotive force in the coil.

diagrams.label: Figure 6.13
diagrams.description: A schematic diagram of an AC generator showing a rectangular coil placed between the North and South poles of a magnet. The coil is attached to an axle and connected to slip rings and carbon brushes, which lead to an external circuit producing alternating emf.

subsections.title: Components and Operation of an AC Generator
subsections.content_text: An AC generator consists of a coil, known as the armature, mounted on a rotor shaft. This armature is mechanically rotated within a stationary, uniform magnetic field by an external power source. The ends of the armature coil are connected to slip rings that rotate with the coil. Carbon brushes press against these slip rings to maintain a continuous electrical connection to the external circuit. The rotation causes the magnetic flux through the coil to change periodically, inducing an alternating emf.

subsections.title: Derivation of Induced EMF in a Rotating Coil
subsections.content_text: Consider a coil with $N$ turns rotating at a constant angular speed $\omega$. The angle $\theta$ at any time $t$ is $\theta = \omega t$ (assuming $\theta = 0$ at $t = 0$). The magnetic flux $\Phi_B$ through the coil is $BA \cos(\omega t)$. According to Faraday's law, the induced emf is $\epsilon = -N \frac{d\Phi_B}{dt} = -NBA \frac{d}{dt}(\cos \omega t)$. Performing the differentiation, we obtain $\epsilon = NBA \omega \sin(\omega t)$.

subsections.title: Sinusoidal Variation of EMF
subsections.content_text: The induced emf follows a sine function: $\epsilon = \epsilon_0 \sin(\omega t)$, where $\epsilon_0 = NBA \omega$ represents the maximum or peak value of the emf. Because the sine function oscillates between $+1$ and $-1$, the polarity of the emf reverses periodically. The emf reaches its maximum magnitude when the change of flux is greatest, which occurs at $\theta = 90^\circ$ and $\theta = 270^\circ$. The current produced also changes direction periodically and is called alternating current (ac). The emf can also be expressed in terms of frequency $\nu$ as $\epsilon = \epsilon_0 \sin(2\pi \nu t)$.

subsections.title: Commercial Power Sources
subsections.content_text: In commercial power plants, the mechanical energy required to rotate the generator's armature is supplied by various means. Hydro-electric generators utilize the energy of falling water. Thermal generators use high-pressure steam produced by burning coal or other fossil fuels. Nuclear power generators use heat from nuclear reactions to produce steam. Modern large-scale generators are capable of producing power outputs as high as 500 MW.

subsections.title: Commercial Generator Specifications
subsections.content_text: up 5 million 100 W bulbs! In most generators, the coils are held stationary and it is the electromagnets which are rotated. The frequency of rotation is 50 Hz in India. In certain countries such as USA, it is 60 Hz.

subsections.title: Stages of EMF Generation in a Rotating Loop
subsections.content_text: The generation of an alternating emf by a loop of wire rotating in a magnetic field can be visualized in five stages. Stage 1: The plane of the armature is perpendicular to the magnetic field; the induced emf is zero. Stage 2: The armature rotates through $90^\circ$, making the plane parallel to the magnetic field; the induced emf reaches its positive peak. Stage 3: After a rotation of $180^\circ$, the plane is again perpendicular; the emf returns to zero. Stage 4: At $270^\circ$, the armature is parallel again but in the opposite orientation; the emf reaches its negative peak. Stage 5: After a full $360^\circ$ rotation, the armature returns to its initial position and the emf is zero. This sinusoidal variation is shown in Figure 6.14.

diagrams.label: FIGURE 6.14
diagrams.description: A diagram showing five stages of a rectangular loop rotating in a magnetic field between North and South poles. Below the stages, a sine wave graph plots induced emf against time/angle, showing zero points at $0^\circ$, $180^\circ$, and $360^\circ$, and peaks at $90^\circ$ ($T/4$) and $270^\circ$ ($3T/4$).

subsections.title: Example 6.10: Maximum Voltage in a Rotating Coil
subsections.content_text: This example demonstrates how to calculate the maximum voltage (peak emf) generated by a rotating coil using the parameters of turns, area, magnetic field, and frequency.

worked_examples.label: Example 6.10
worked_examples.problem: Kamla peddles a stationary bicycle. The pedals of the bicycle are attached to a 100 turn coil of area 0.10 m$^2$. The coil rotates at half a revolution per second and it is placed in a uniform magnetic field of 0.01 T perpendicular to the axis of rotation of the coil. What is the maximum voltage generated in the coil?
worked_examples.solution: Here $\nu = 0.5$ Hz; $N = 100$, $A = 0.1$ m$^2$ and $B = 0.01$ T. Employing Eq. (6.19): $\varepsilon_0 = NBA (2 \pi \nu) = 100 \times 0.01 \times 0.1 \times 2 \times 3.14 \times 0.5 = 0.314$ V. The maximum voltage is 0.314 V.

subsections.title: Key Principles and Laws
subsections.content_text: The chapter on electromagnetic induction is summarized by several fundamental laws and definitions: 1. Magnetic flux $\Phi_B$ through a surface of area $\mathbf{A}$ in a field $\mathbf{B}$ is $\Phi_B = \mathbf{B} \cdot \mathbf{A} = BA \cos \theta$. 2. Faraday's laws state that induced emf $\varepsilon = -N \frac{d\Phi_B}{dt}$. 3. Lenz's law states the polarity of induced emf opposes the change in magnetic flux. 4. Motional emf for a rod of length $l$ moving at velocity $v$ perpendicular to field $B$ is $\varepsilon = Blv$. 5. Inductance is the ratio of flux-linkage to current ($N\Phi/I$). 6. Mutual inductance $M_{12}$ relates emf in coil 1 to current change in coil 2: $\varepsilon_1 = -M_{12} \frac{dI_2}{dt}$. 7. Self-induced emf is $\varepsilon = -L \frac{dI}{dt}$, where $L$ is self-inductance. 8. For a long solenoid, $L = \mu_r \mu_0 n^2 Al$. 9. In an AC generator, the motional emf is $\varepsilon = NBA (2\pi\nu) \sin (2\pi\nu t)$.

tables.label: Physical Quantities in Electromagnetic Induction
tables.caption: Summary of symbols, units, dimensions, and equations for key quantities.

subsections.title: Theoretical Insights
subsections.content_text: 1. Electricity and magnetism are intimately related; moving charges produce magnetic fields, and moving magnets can induce electric currents. 2. In closed circuits, induced currents oppose the change in flux, consistent with the law of conservation of energy. In open circuits, an emf is still induced across the ends. 3. Motional emf can be derived from the Lorentz force on moving charges. However, even for stationary charges, a time-varying magnetic field induces an emf, suggesting a symmetry that hints at the principle of relativity.

subsections.title: Visualizing Electromagnetic Induction
subsections.content_text: The following exercises involve applying Lenz's law and Faraday's law to various physical configurations. Figure 6.15 illustrates several scenarios where the direction of induced current must be predicted based on changes in magnetic flux. Figure 6.16 focuses on the effects of changing the shape and area of a loop within a uniform magnetic field. These problems test the fundamental principle that an induced current always flows in a direction such that it opposes the change in magnetic flux that produced it.

diagrams.label: FIGURE 6.15
diagrams.description: A series of six diagrams (a-f) showing different electromagnetic induction scenarios: (a) A bar magnet moving towards a solenoid; (b) A bar magnet moving between two solenoids; (c) Two coaxial loops with a tapping key being closed; (d) Two coaxial loops with a rheostat setting being changed; (e) Two solenoids with a tapping key being released; (f) A straight wire with decreasing current near a horizontal loop.

diagrams.label: FIGURE 6.16
diagrams.description: Two diagrams showing loops in magnetic fields: (a) An irregular loop expanding into a circle in a field directed into the page; (b) A circular loop contracting into a narrow wire in a field directed out of the page.

items.problem: Predict the direction of induced current in the situations described by the following Figs. 6.15(a) to (f). Predict the direction of induced current in the situations described by the following Figs. 6.15(a) to (f).

Use Lenz’s law to determine the direction of induced current in the situations described by Fig. 6.16: (a) A wire of irregular shape turning into a circular shape; (b) A circular loop being deformed into a narrow straight wire.
items.solution: (a) The direction is $adcb$ (anticlockwise). As the area increases, the flux (into the page) increases; the induced current creates a field out of the page to oppose this. (b) The direction is $a'b'c'd'$ (anticlockwise). As the area decreases, the flux (out of the page) decreases; the induced current creates a field out of the page to support the existing flux.

items.problem: A long solenoid with $15$ turns per cm has a small loop of area $2.0 \text{ cm}^2$ placed inside the solenoid normal to its axis. If the current carried by the solenoid changes steadily from $2.0 \text{ A}$ to $4.0 \text{ A}$ in $0.1 \text{ s}$, what is the induced emf in the loop while the current is changing?
items.solution: Given $n = 1500 \text{ turns/m}$, $A = 2.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$, and $\frac{dI}{dt} = \frac{4.0 - 2.0}{0.1} = 20 \text{ A/s}$. The induced emf is $e = \frac{d\phi}{dt} = A \mu_0 n \frac{dI}{dt}$. Substituting values: $e = (2.0 \times 10^{-4}) \times (4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \times 1500 \times 20 \approx 7.54 \times 10^{-6} \text{ V}$.

items.problem: A rectangular wire loop of sides $8 \text{ cm}$ and $2 \text{ cm}$ with a small cut is moving out of a region of uniform magnetic field of magnitude $0.3 \text{ T}$ directed normal to the loop. What is the emf developed across the cut if the velocity of the loop is $1 \text{ cm s}^{-1}$ in a direction normal to the (a) longer side, (b) shorter side of the loop? For how long does the induced voltage last in each case?
items.solution: (a) $e = Blv = 0.3 \times 0.08 \times 0.01 = 2.4 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V}$. The voltage lasts for $t = \frac{w}{v} = \frac{0.02}{0.01} = 2 \text{ s}$. (b) $e = Blv = 0.3 \times 0.02 \times 0.01 = 0.6 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V}$. The voltage lasts for $t = \frac{l}{v} = \frac{0.08}{0.01} = 8 \text{ s}$.

items.problem: A $1.0 \text{ m}$ long metallic rod is rotated with an angular frequency of $400 \text{ rad s}^{-1}$ about an axis normal to the rod passing through its one end. The other end of the rod is in contact with a circular metallic ring. A constant and uniform magnetic field of $0.5 \text{ T}$ parallel to the axis exists everywhere. Calculate the emf developed between the centre and the ring.
items.solution: The induced emf for a rotating rod is $e = \frac{1}{2} B \omega L^2$. Substituting $B = 0.5 \text{ T}$, $\omega = 400 \text{ rad/s}$, and $L = 1.0 \text{ m}$: $e = \frac{1}{2} \times 0.5 \times 400 \times (1.0)^2 = 100 \text{ V}$.

items.problem: A horizontal straight wire $10 \text{ m}$ long extending from east to west is falling with a speed of $5.0 \text{ m s}^{-1}$, at right angles to the horizontal component of the earth’s magnetic field, $0.30 \times 10^{-4} \text{ Wb m}^{-2}$. (a) What is the instantaneous value of the emf induced in the wire? (b) What is the direction of the emf? (c) Which end of the wire is at the higher electrical potential?
items.solution: (a) $e = Blv = (0.30 \times 10^{-4}) \times 10 \times 5.0 = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V}$. (b) By Fleming's Right Hand Rule, the direction of induced current is from West to East. (c) The Eastern end is at a higher electrical potential.

items.problem: Current in a circuit falls from $5.0 \text{ A}$ to $0.0 \text{ A}$ in $0.1 \text{ s}$. If an average emf of $200 \text{ V}$ induced, give an estimate of the self-inductance of the circuit.
items.solution: Using $e = L \left| \frac{dI}{dt} \right|$, we have $200 = L \frac{5.0 - 0.0}{0.1}$. Thus, $200 = 50L$, which gives $L = 4 \text{ H}$.

items.problem: A pair of adjacent coils has a mutual inductance of $1.5 \text{ H}$. If the current in one coil changes from $0$ to $20 \text{ A}$ in $0.5 \text{ s}$, what is the change of flux linkage with the other coil?
items.solution: The change in flux linkage is $\Delta \phi = M \Delta I$. Given $M = 1.5 \text{ H}$ and $\Delta I = 20 \text{ A}$, $\Delta \phi = 1.5 \times 20 = 30 \text{ Wb}$.
